prompt: German cockroaches are best identified by:

options.text: Small size and two dark longitudinal stripes on the pronotum
Being the size of a mouse with no markings
Producing light like fireflies
Having only four legs

explanation: Blattella germanica is small, light brown, with two pronotal stripes—common indoor pest.

prompt: Bed bugs typically feed on:

options.text: Only wood cellulose like termites
Blood of humans (and other hosts) usually at night
Only stored flour
Only living plant leaves outdoors

explanation: Cimex lectularius are hematophagous ectoparasites associated with sleeping areas.

distractorExplanations.a: Wood feeding is termites/carpenter ants context.
distractorExplanations.d: Bed bugs are not plant chewers.

prompt: Subterranean termite workers are generally:

options.text: Hard-bodied beetles that fly into kitchens for crumbs only
Bright red with black spots like lady beetles
Soft-bodied, light-colored, and avoid open air/light by using mud tubes
Aquatic nymphs that need ponds

explanation: Subterranean termites maintain contact with soil moisture and build shelter tubes.

distractorExplanations.a: That describes pantry beetles more than termites.

prompt: Carpenter ants damage wood by:

options.text: Digesting cellulose with protozoa exactly like subterranean termites
Feeding only on blood
Never nesting in structures
Excavating galleries for nesting (they do not eat wood for nutrition like termites)

explanation: Carpenter ants smooth galleries in wood for housing; frass may be present; termites consume wood.

distractorExplanations.a: Termites digest cellulose; carpenter ants excavate.

prompt: Rodent droppings that are capsule-shaped (~¾ inch) suggest:

options.text: Norway rats more than house mice
Fruit flies
German cockroaches exclusively
Bed bug fecal spots only

explanation: Norway rat pellets are larger; mouse droppings are smaller rods; ID guides treatment.

distractorExplanations.d: Bed bug feces are dark spots on fabrics/wood.

prompt: Anopheles mosquitoes are important because some species transmit:

options.text: Only termites
Malaria parasites
Only lead poisoning
Only structural wood decay fungi as the vector

explanation: Anopheles vectors Plasmodium; control reduces disease risk in endemic/relevant contexts.

distractorExplanations.d: Decay fungi are not mosquito diseases.

prompt: Complete metamorphosis includes which stages?

options.text: Egg and adult only
Nymph stages identical to adults only (incomplete)
Egg, larva, pupa, adult
Spore, hypha, mushroom

explanation: Holometabolous pests (fleas, flies, beetles, mosquitoes) have larval/pupal stages.

distractorExplanations.a: Many pests have more than two stages.

prompt: Stored-product pests such as Indian meal moths are typically found in:

options.text: Only septic tanks
Only structural lumber in crawlspaces
Only on living oak trees outdoors as borers exclusively
Pantry goods like grains, cereals, and dried foods

explanation: Pantry moths/beetles infest packaged dry foods; sanitation and discard are key.

prompt: Brown recluse spiders are medically important because of:

options.text: Necrotic potential from venom in some bites
Transmitting malaria
Eating only concrete
Being harmless millipedes

explanation: Loxosceles reclusa venom can cause dermonecrosis; ID and medical care matter—many 'recluse' IDs are wrong.

prompt: Flea larvae typically develop:

options.text: Only inside the host's bloodstream as adults permanently
Off-host in carpets/soil/bedding debris feeding on organic matter including adult flea feces
Only in clean empty stainless steel with no debris
Only underwater in aquaria

explanation: IPM targets eggs/larvae in the environment, not just adult fleas on pets.

distractorExplanations.d: Fleas are not aquatic aquaria pests.

prompt: Powderpost beetles are associated with:

options.text: Building mud tubes from soil to siding exclusively like subterranean termites
Feeding only on blood at night
Infesting hardwoods and leaving fine flour-like frass from exit holes
Pollinating crops as bees

explanation: Lyctid/anobiid powderpost activity shows pinholes and powdery frass in susceptible wood.

prompt: House mouse distinguishing traits include:

options.text: Body length over two feet
Hooves
Feathers
Small size, large ears relative to head, and droppings that are small and rod-shaped

explanation: Mus musculus is small with characteristic droppings and gnaw marks; ID guides bait station placement.

distractorExplanations.a: That size is not a mouse.

prompt: Drift of pest ID errors matters because:

options.text: Wrong ID leads to wrong tactics, wasted pesticide, and continued infestation
All pests respond identically to any product
Labels never mention target pests
ID is optional for certified applicators

explanation: Correct identification is the foundation of IPM and legal labeled use.

prompt: Formosan subterranean termites differ from many native subterranean species by:

options.text: Being beneficial pollinators only
Often larger colonies and aggressive structural damage potential in introduced ranges
Living only in arctic ice
Never using carton/nests in structures

explanation: Coptotermes formosanus can cause severe damage; regional knowledge informs inspection intensity.

distractorExplanations.d: They can build carton nests in structures.

prompt: Mosquito larvae (wrigglers) are found in:

options.text: Only dry flour bags
Only kiln-dried lumber interiors with no moisture
Standing water habitats
Only sealed sterile vials in labs as the sole habitat worldwide

explanation: Source reduction of standing water is primary mosquito IPM.

distractorExplanations.b: Dry lumber is not larval mosquito habitat.

prompt: The pesticide label is:

options.text: A legal document—use inconsistent with the label is generally a violation
Optional reading material
Only a marketing brochure
Suggestions that never matter in inspections

explanation: FIFRA makes the label the law; applicators must follow directions, rates, PPE, and site uses.

distractorExplanations.c: Labels are regulatory, not just ads.

prompt: LD50 refers to:

options.text: The legal discount of 50% on products
The dose lethal to 50% of a test population—lower LD50 means higher acute toxicity
Days until a pesticide expires always
The percentage of inert ingredients only

explanation: Acute toxicity categories use LD50/LC50 concepts; signal words reflect relative hazard.

distractorExplanations.d: Inerts are listed separately; LD50 is toxicity metric.

prompt: Signal word DANGER on a label indicates:

options.text: The product is always nontoxic
No PPE is ever needed
High acute toxicity / high hazard relative to CAUTION/WARNING categories
It is safer than CAUTION products always by definition

explanation: DANGER (sometimes with Poison/skull) denotes highest acute toxicity category among common signal words.

distractorExplanations.b: PPE is specified on the label.
distractorExplanations.d: CAUTION is lower acute toxicity category than DANGER.

prompt: IPM emphasizes:

options.text: Calendar spraying without inspection forever
Using the highest rate possible always
Ignoring harborage and sanitation
Combining inspection, sanitation, exclusion, and pesticides only as needed

explanation: Integrated Pest Management reduces reliance on chemicals by addressing root causes.

distractorExplanations.a: Blind calendars waste pesticide and select resistance.

prompt: A residual insecticide is intended to:

options.text: Remain active on treated surfaces for a period to kill pests contacting residues
Evaporate instantly with no surface activity
Act only as a fertilizer
Neutralize all odors permanently

explanation: Residuals differ from contact-only/space sprays; placement and surface type affect performance.

distractorExplanations.b: Residuals persist by design (within label limits).
distractorExplanations.d: Odor control is not the mode of action definition.

prompt: Bait formulations work best when:

options.text: Sprayed as a broadcast fog over food prep surfaces contrary to label
Competing food is reduced and baits are placed in areas of pest activity per label
Mixed into family dinners
Placed only in unreachable voids with zero pest activity signs

explanation: Baits compete with other food; sanitation and correct placement drive success.

distractorExplanations.a: Fogging baits onto food surfaces is misuse.

prompt: Herbicide exposure risk to an applicator is an example of concern about:

options.text: Only the color of the bottle cap as toxicity
Wind direction never mattering
Pesticide toxicity and route of entry (dermal, inhalation, oral)
PPE being purely fashion

explanation: Understanding toxicity and exposure routes guides PPE and handling—even for non-insecticides.

distractorExplanations.a: Cap color is not the toxicity measure.

prompt: Resistance management may include:

options.text: Using the same product daily forever at sublethal rates intentionally
Never reading mode-of-action information
Applying below-label rates to 'save money' routinely
Rotating modes of action and integrating nonchemical tactics

explanation: Repeated identical MOAs select resistant populations; IPM slows resistance.

distractorExplanations.a: Sublethal chronic exposure can select resistance.

prompt: Inert ingredients on a label:

options.text: Are not necessarily harmless—follow the label; trade secrets may limit naming but hazards still matter
Are always water and sugar only
Can be ignored for PPE decisions always
Make the product non-regulated

explanation: Inerts can be solvents/carriers with hazards; the full label/SDS informs safe use.

prompt: A restricted-use pesticide (RUP) generally may be applied only by:

options.text: Any untrained homeowner without rules
Certified applicators (or persons under their direct supervision as allowed by law)
Children as a school project
Anyone who finds an unmarked bottle

explanation: RUPs require certification due to higher risk; supervision rules are state-specific.

distractorExplanations.a: RUPs are not general-use for anyone.
distractorExplanations.d: Unmarked bottles are illegal to use.

prompt: Synergists in insecticide formulations are used to:

options.text: Feed beneficial bees exclusively
Neutralize the active ingredient intentionally
Enhance toxicity of the active ingredient (e.g., by inhibiting insect detox enzymes)
Act as the only food attractant in all baits

explanation: Piperonyl butoxide-type synergists can increase efficacy of some actives.

prompt: Contact vs systemic pesticides differ in that systemics:

options.text: Never leave the spray droplet on the surface conceptually
Are identical to inert dusts only
Cannot appear on any label
Are taken up and move within the treated plant/animal system as designed

explanation: Systemic movement affects what pests are controlled and residue considerations; structural uses vary by product.

distractorExplanations.b: Dusts can be contact; systemics are a property of actives/formulations.

prompt: Emulsifiable concentrates (ECs) mixed with water form:

options.text: Emulsions that can be more readily absorbed through skin—handle with care
Gases only
Solids that cannot spill
Harmless drinking water

explanation: ECs often use solvents; dermal absorption risk can be significant—PPE per label.

prompt: The SDS (Safety Data Sheet) provides:

options.text: Only coupon codes
Detailed hazard, first aid, spill, and handling information for the product
Permission to ignore the label
Guaranteed nontoxicity certificates

explanation: SDS complements the label for occupational hazard communication (HazCom).

distractorExplanations.d: SDS describes hazards, not 'safe to ignore'.

prompt: Choosing the least hazardous effective option is part of:

options.text: Always maximizing toxicity for marketing
Ignoring nonchemical options
IPM and responsible pesticide selection
Using unlabeled homemade concentrates

explanation: Match product to pest/site with efficacy and risk reduction; homemade mixes are illegal/dangerous.

distractorExplanations.a: Higher toxicity is not a goal.
distractorExplanations.b: Nonchemical tactics come first when effective.

prompt: Before spraying indoors, an applicator should:

options.text: Read the label, prepare the site (cover/remove items as directed), and wear required PPE
Spray first and read later
Mix by smell
Disable smoke detectors to avoid beeps

explanation: Preparation and PPE prevent exposure and contamination of food/utensils/toys.

prompt: The most common route of pesticide exposure for applicators is often:

options.text: Telepathy
Dermal (skin) absorption
Exposure only through fingernails growing
None—PPE makes exposure impossible always

explanation: Skin contact during mixing/loading/application is a primary exposure pathway; gloves/coveralls matter.

distractorExplanations.d: PPE reduces—not magically eliminates—all risk if misused.

prompt: When mixing pesticides, correct practice is to:

options.text: Siphon by mouth
Mix in kitchen cooking pots used for food
Wear label-required PPE, measure accurately, and avoid splash by proper pouring/order
Estimate rates as 'about a glug'

explanation: Accurate measurement and anti-splash technique prevent acute exposures and illegal rates.

distractorExplanations.b: Food utensils must never be used.

prompt: If pesticide splashes in the eyes, first aid generally starts with:

options.text: Rubbing eyes with dirty gloves
Waiting a day to see if it improves without rinsing
Applying more concentrate to 'neutralize'
Immediate rinsing with clean water for the label/SDS recommended duration while seeking medical help as directed

explanation: Prompt irrigation is critical; follow SDS/label and get medical care with product information.

prompt: Respirator use requires:

options.text: Correct respirator type for the hazard, fit, and cartridge change schedules per guidance
Any dust mask for fumigants always
Facial hair that breaks the seal as ideal
Sharing cartridges soaked in pesticide as reuse best practice

explanation: NIOSH-approved respirators must match the chemical; fit and maintenance are mandatory.

distractorExplanations.b: Fumigants often need supplied-air/SCBA—not dust masks.
distractorExplanations.d: Contaminated cartridges are hazardous waste/not shared.

prompt: To reduce spray drift outdoors:

options.text: Spray during the windiest part of the day intentionally
Avoid high winds, use appropriate nozzles/pressure, and respect buffers
Use the finest possible mist in gusts
Ignore sensitive sites downwind

explanation: Drift injures nontargets and creates illegal off-site residues.

prompt: After application, PPE should be:

options.text: Worn home to hug family before washing
Washed with dishes in the kitchen sink routinely
Removed carefully to avoid contaminating skin, then cleaned or disposed per label
Never cleaned

explanation: Doffing sequence and separate laundry prevent take-home exposure.

distractorExplanations.b: Kitchen sinks are for food hygiene, not PPE.

prompt: Closed mixing/loading systems are designed to:

options.text: Increase spills intentionally
Eliminate the need to read labels
Allow mouth siphoning safely
Reduce handler exposure during concentrate transfer

explanation: Engineering controls complement PPE for concentrate handling.

prompt: When a spill occurs, priorities include:

options.text: Protect people, stop/contain the spill if safe, and clean up per label/SDS and regulations
Hosing concentrates into storm drains immediately
Leaving the spill unattended in a public aisle
Lighting a match to 'burn it off' indoors

explanation: Spill response prevents environmental release and secondary exposures; never create fire/explosion hazards.

prompt: Calibration of application equipment ensures:

options.text: Random over-application as a buffer always
The correct labeled rate is delivered
That gauges never need checking
Legal immunity if you guess

explanation: Uncalibrated equipment causes under-/over-application—efficacy and compliance suffer.

prompt: Space treatments (fogs/mists) require special caution because:

options.text: They never move in air
They are always safer than baits for occupied nurseries without precautions
Airborne droplets increase inhalation exposure and may require vacating/reentry intervals
PPE is never specified for fogs

explanation: ULV/fogging can expose lungs; follow ventilation and reentry directions strictly.

distractorExplanations.b: Occupied sensitive sites need careful method selection.

prompt: Heat stress risk for applicators rises when:

options.text: Working only in cool shade with breaks always eliminates all legal duties
PPE is unnecessary in heat
Salt tablets replace water without medical guidance always
Wearing impermeable PPE in hot environments without work/rest/hydration planning

explanation: PPE increases heat load; employers/applicators must manage heat illness risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Cool conditions help but duties remain.
distractorExplanations.b: PPE may still be required—manage heat differently.
distractorExplanations.c: Hydration strategies should be appropriate; medical guidance matters.

prompt: Triple rinsing empty pesticide containers is done to:

options.text: Remove residues so containers can be disposed/recycled per regulations
Make containers safe as drinking cups
Avoid pressure-rinsing when the label requires proper rinsing
Pour rinsate into creeks

explanation: Rinsate goes into the spray tank when allowed; containers are punctured/recycled per rules—never reused for food/water.

distractorExplanations.c: Proper rinsing (including pressure rinse) is required.

prompt: A symptom of moderate pesticide poisoning may include:

options.text: Feeling heroic and doubling workload without reporting
Nausea, sweating, headache, or other label/SDS-listed effects—seek medical help and take the label/SDS
Assuming all symptoms are allergies to gloves only without evaluation
Drinking milk as proven universal antidote for all pesticide classes

explanation: Recognizing poisoning and getting medical care with product identity saves lives; antidotes are chemical-specific.

distractorExplanations.d: No universal home antidote exists for all pesticides.

prompt: When two products are tank-mixed, you must:

options.text: Mix any two concentrates because stronger is better
Ignore proprietary tank-mix prohibitions
Ensure label allows mixing and follow compatibility/order instructions
Heat mixtures in a closed bottle over flame

explanation: Incompatible mixes can clog equipment, reduce efficacy, or create hazards; labels govern.

distractorExplanations.d: Heating closed pesticide containers risks explosion.

prompt: FIFRA is the federal law that primarily:

options.text: Regulates pesticide registration, labeling, and sale/use in the United States
Sets only restaurant tip wages
Regulates airline ticket prices
Bans all state authority forever

explanation: The Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act is the core federal pesticide statute; states may be stricter.

prompt: Applying a pesticide to a site not listed on the label is:

options.text: Encouraged creativity
Generally illegal (unless a specific legal exemption applies)
Required for certification CEU
Allowed if the pest looks similar

explanation: Site/pest must be on the label; similarity is not a legal substitute.

distractorExplanations.d: Similar pests still need labeled uses.

prompt: Pesticide storage should be:

options.text: In an unlocked toy chest at home
Next to lunch in the truck cab cupholder
Secure, ventilated as appropriate, locked from children/pets, and segregated from food/feed
Outdoors unlabeled in the rain without secondary containment when required

explanation: Storage rules prevent theft, spills, and accidental exposures; follow label and state rules.

distractorExplanations.d: Weathering/unlabeled storage creates hazards and violations.

prompt: Records of commercial applications are important because they:

options.text: Are illegal to keep
Replace the need for a label
Must be destroyed daily always
Document compliance and support investigations if problems arise

explanation: Many jurisdictions require application records (product, rate, site, date, applicator).

distractorExplanations.c: Retention periods apply—do not destroy improperly.

prompt: Endangered species and pollinator protections on labels may require:

options.text: Following geographic/use restrictions and timing/method limits to protect nontargets
Ignoring bee icons because they are decorative
Spraying blooming plants attractive to bees contrary to label precautions
Dumping leftovers in habitat areas

explanation: EPA/state mitigations protect listed species and bees; label advisories/restrictions are enforceable.

prompt: Backflow prevention when filling tanks from water supplies is required to:

options.text: Increase siphon risk intentionally
Prevent pesticide from siphoning into public/private water systems
Flavor drinking water
Satisfy only aesthetic preferences

explanation: Air gaps/backflow devices protect water supplies during mixing/loading.

prompt: Container disposal must follow:

options.text: Burning all containers in backyard piles always as best practice
Burying concentrates in the woods
Label directions and hazardous waste/recycling rules—never reuse for water/food
Pouring leftovers down sanitary drains casually

explanation: Improper disposal contaminates environment and violates law.

distractorExplanations.b: Burial of concentrates is illegal dumping.
distractorExplanations.d: Drains lead to treatment plants/waterways issues.

prompt: State certification of applicators exists to:

options.text: Replace federal labels entirely
Allow uncertified RUP use freely
Eliminate CE requirements in all states forever
Ensure minimum competency for safe, legal pesticide use

explanation: Certification/licensing demonstrates knowledge; CE maintains competency—details vary by state.

prompt: Transporting pesticides safely includes:

options.text: Securing containers upright, protecting labels, and separating from passengers/food as required
Loose containers sliding in the back seat with kids
Unlabeled jugs only
Venting concentrates into the cab heater

explanation: DOT/state rules and common sense prevent spills and exposure during transport.

prompt: Falsifying application records is:

options.text: A recommended shortcut
Illegal and can result in penalties/license actions
Required when you forget what you applied
Harmless because records are never reviewed

explanation: Honest records are a compliance obligation; guesswork should be replaced by better documentation habits.

distractorExplanations.c: Forgetting is not fixed by fraud.

prompt: Community right-to-know / posting & notification rules may require:

options.text: Secret night spraying to avoid all notice always when notice is required
Never telling property managers anything
Advance notice or posting for certain applications per state/local ordinance
Removing posted signs immediately before reentry times end when posting is required

explanation: Schools, apartments, and some municipalities have notification/posting laws beyond the label.

distractorExplanations.a: Required notice cannot be evaded by secrecy.
distractorExplanations.d: Signs stay up for required periods.

prompt: WPS (Worker Protection Standard) primarily protects:

options.text: Only office software users
Only deep-sea fishermen from storms
Structural applicators from all city parking tickets
Agricultural workers and pesticide handlers on covered agricultural establishments

explanation: WPS is an EPA agricultural worker rule; structural work has other OSHA/state frameworks—but know which rules apply to your job.

prompt: Groundwater advisories on labels exist because some chemicals:

options.text: Can leach and contaminate groundwater under certain soil/rainfall conditions
Always float harmlessly forever
Cannot move in soil ever
Are required to be poured into wells

explanation: Vulnerable aquifers and sandy soils increase leaching risk—follow setbacks and rates.

distractorExplanations.b: Many pesticides can move with water.
distractorExplanations.c: Soil mobility varies but is real for some AIs.

prompt: An applicator finds an old unlabeled pesticide container. Best action:

options.text: Guess it is water and spray interiors
Treat as unknown hazardous material—do not use; contact authorities/hazardous waste channels for disposal guidance
Mix with a new product to 'identify' by smell
Give it to neighbors as free samples

explanation: Unlabeled pesticides cannot be used legally/safely; manage as hazardous waste per guidance.

prompt: Civil/criminal penalties for pesticide misuse can include:

options.text: Only a thank-you letter
Automatic immunity for commercial applicators
Fines, license suspension/revocation, and in serious cases criminal charges
Free product forever

explanation: Misuse harming people, wildlife, or environment can bring severe enforcement consequences.
